sentences.text: I was named after my maternal great-grandmother, [?
Itka ?] [INAUDIBLE], who was also born in Poland, and was an Orthodox Jewish woman.
Her husband was [INAUDIBLE] rabbi [INAUDIBLE].
And she was a wonderful woman, so I'm proud to have her name.
That's my-- my legal name is Itka.
My family, of blessed memory, used to call Itkele, endearing for Itka.
And I loved to be called that name, Itkele.
I was born during Passover, April 15, 1926, in a small town Ciechonw, Poland.
Ciechonw, C-I-E-C-H-O-N-W.
Ciechonw, Poland, located about 90 kilometer north of Warsaw.
Today, God willing, on April 15, going to be 70 years old.
At birth, my birth name was [?
Ital ?], you know, the Hebrew name, and Itka.
That's what I have in the paper, as I mentioned.
My last name, Frajman.
F-R-A-J-M-A-N.
Before the Holocaust, our household consisted of my parents, Simo and Eljo Frajman, my maternal widowed grandmother, Chaye Sure Zebrak, my younger brother, Srulek, and my little sister, Zeisel.
No.
Itkele, everybody called me, because my great-grandmother had many great-grandchildren named after her.
And since some were older than I, so my family usually was calling everyone, you know, that diminutive name.
My Hebrew name is [?
Ital. ?]
When I, before the Holocaust, I had a dream.
I was dreaming of getting a good formal education and of becoming a writer.
Ever since I started to go school, I used to write little stories and poems.
But instead of a writer, as you know, it's, it's obvious, I became a Holocaust survivor.
And all my dreams-- that Hitler's dream became my nightmares.
However, he couldn't take away my creativity and my dreams.
And this is a poem that I wrote on May 18, 1978.
It is called 'A World That Vanished.' 'Just like a leaf tossed to the wind, separated from its branches and tree, not belonging anymore, liberated, but not free.
Carrying the pain of separation.
Memories so painful, yet dear.
Memories of a world that used to be, a world that is no longer here.
I still see the Sabbath candles and my mother's gentle face.
My father is chanting the prayers, my grandmother with her white shawl of lace.
I see my little brother and sister, so young and so full of cheer, not yet understanding prejudice and hatred, but sensing uncertainty and fears.
The Nazi boots are marching my world, with terrorists filled.
The holy scriptures are burning.
Children are starved, tortured, and killed.
The ghettos are closing us in.
What is there for us in store?
My world is growing smaller and smaller.
Nothing is like it was before.
The freight trains are taking us on a journey.
For most, it is their last ride.
My brothers and sisters are burning.
There is no more place to hide.
Hitler's final solution is sealed.
We are trapped in a world of hell.
I am amazed that I have survived, the story of the Holocaust to tell.'
My father's name was Eljo.
E-L-J-O.
I spell it like in Polish was spelled.
My mother's name was Simo.
My grandmother's, Chaye Sure.
What I remember about my father, I remember my, my parents-- before my parents were married, my mother played in the amateur Yiddish theater.
In fact, that's how I met-- they met, because my father was born in Pultusk, Poland.
My mother was born in Siedlec, Poland.
But when she came with a drama group-- in fact, I have even a picture that I retrieved from someone-- they got to know each other and obviously got married.
And if-- but after my mother was married and she had a family, her priority was her home and her family.
And so she would only perform for, for, tzedakah, for charity.
But she never lost the interest in the Yiddish theater.
And she had a lovely voice.
And both my father and I used to sing.
So what I remember a lot is how they used to sing.
In fact, I can still remember my mother's feel, like my mother's cheek, like when she would put me to bed.
Or sometimes, my father would sing me Yiddish lullabies, Yiddish songs.
Because in our home, even though both my parents and my grandmother could speak fluently Polish and Yiddish, we only spoke Yiddish at home.
So they taught me lots of Yiddish songs.
Another thing I remember about my father, he would always ask us, so what did you learn, my child?
Which made me aware that there is a lesson in every experience.
And also, I remember that we had-- I felt loved by my parents.
I felt that both my parents and my grandmother loved me unconditionally.
But, they also were strict with us.
From early childhood on, both my parents and my grandmother stressed to us the importance of menschlichkeit, which in the English language means humaneness.

Whenever I wouldn't do something which would measure up to the yardstick of menschlichkeit, they would always used to say, 'Itkele, it's not menschlich.' And that used to helped me more than if they would physically hit me, because I felt like I let them down.
And eventually, I started to measure my own behavior by the yardstick of menschlichkeit.
Once, I had an experience, a very painful, actually, experience, which I must-- that was like not too far away before the Holocaust.
Because for me, the Holocaust started at age 13, when Germany invaded Poland.
I was walking home from school alone, because I had both Hebrew, I mean, Yiddish education and, and went to public school.
And I was walking home from public school, and a bunch of non-Jewish kids-- kids that I didn't even know or didn't know me-- attacked me both verbally and physically.
Called me-- called me terrible names.
I feel-- 'Christ killer' and 'you Jew.' All kinds of names.
Go up to Palestine.
Go to, you know, very, very insulting names.
And I tried to free myself, but the circle closed around me and I couldn't.
And I felt so horrible because I realized, why?
Why do they hate me?
How can you hate somebody you don't even know?
I couldn't understand it.
And I came home crying bitterly.
Normally, I didn't cry just for any little thing, so my mother was at that time home.
So she asked me, 'Itkele, why are you crying?' And I told her.
Surprisingly, she did not make any comment.
She just took me lovingly in her arms and she tried to comfort me.
And after a while, when my sobs quiet down, she looked at me seriously, and she asked me, 'So what did you do, my child?' And I was surprised, because I figured, what could I do?
I was all alone.
I didn't do anything.

So I said, 'Nothing, Mom.' And my mother said reassuringly, 'Well, Itkele, then you have nothing to cry about, because your menschlichkeit does not depend on how others treat you, but on how you treat others.' My parents taught me never to hurt or shame anyone, that that's an [NON-ENGLISH], which means a sin.
But they never, unfortunately, taught me what to do when somebody hurts me.
So the only thing I could do when somebody hurt me was crying.
If-- and-- and everything was measured, like I said, by the yardstick of menschlichkeit.
When we lived in Poland, physically, our lives were very hard.
For instance, we didn't have-- in our, our hometown, where I lived before the Holocaust, we didn't have electricity in our home.
We didn't have running water.
We didn't have plumbing, indoor plumbing.
So those things affected very much.
Was very difficult.
We have to, you know.
But since it was a little city, because of those conditions, for instance, then there was a water carrier, that would bring water to the, you know-- or we would have to fetch water ourselves.
We didn't have electricity.
Therefore, we didn't have any of the modern appliances.
And therefore, we didn't have where to store our food in the summer, it wouldn't get-- so we had a dark root cellar.
They used to call it 'root cellar' because some people would buy for the winter a sack of potatoes and onions, you know, store it.
And also, you would have to go down for to bring up and down the food.
One time, I remember my mother asked me to go down and bring up the butter from the root cellar, and she saw that I was afraid.
So she looked at me and she said, 'Go, go, Itkele.
God is in the root cellar too.' And she said she'll wait for me.
She lit a candle.
She gave me the candle to go down.
And the root cellar was dark.
It had a dirt floor, no windows, a long ladder.
To go down-- it was in the kitchen-- you had to-- and an iron ring, it was a square cut out.
You would have to pull up the ring, you know, the square, and to walk down.
And somehow, my mother's reassurance, and having the candle, and mostly, she said, 'Go, go.
God's in the root cellar too.' I don't know.
For some reason, it gave me the courage.
It diminished my fear.
And I went down, and I brought up the butter.
And after that, I was not so afraid anymore to go down to the root cellar, because I was always-- my mother-- the echo of my mother's voice was still in my head. 'Go, go, Itkele.
God is in the root cellar.' In fact, during concentration camp, I was often thinking about this comment.
And I was thinking, when I was in Poland, we didn't have electricity.
But neither did we have electric charge wires when we were fenced in and couldn't go out.
So-- and then, also, there was, for instance, Passover I remember very much.
For one thing, I was born during Passover, you know, the Jewish holiday celebrating freedom.
And I remember Passover was very beautiful in our home, because we had a large-- lots of relatives.
My aunt and uncle had seven children, and they had already grandchildren.
And the relatives would used to go together.

But what I remember most is that it was completely because of the inconveniences of lacking the things, which I said, the modern things, my mother and grandmother-- may they rest in peace-- used to buy beets.
And before the, before the holiday, so like a month before, make beets.
Buy raisin, make their own raisin wine, because it was strictly kosher in our home.
Prepare.
And we used to go, you know, in a, in a pillow case we would put in and go bring the matza-- a special pillow case.
It was not a pillow case that you slept, but, like, it looked like a pillow case.
Bring home the matza for, for yuntif.
And at the seder, I used to love the way they did the seder.
And-- but to tell you the truth, if every year we prayed, next year in Jerusalem.
But the point is, to me, it was like a story.
I believed it, but I, I was still fortunate that I still had a family.
I never knew what hunger was, because I wasn't 13 years-- I was 13 when the war started.
So till 13, I didn't fast for Yom Kippur, so I didn't know what hunger is, you know, because I haven't experienced it.
So it was a story with all the plagues, with all the [INAUDIBLE].
So till-- and later on, I was thinking, when I was-- during the Holocaust, realizing that now I know it's not just a story.
Now I experienced far worse than what it was.
Another thing which I loved that my parents used to take me to the Yiddish theater.
As I mentioned before, my mother would only give a performance occasionally.
So--
What type?
In Yiddish theater.
And I remember that we went to-- the play that I'm telling now, I didn't see her perform in that play.
But they took me much to the theater.
It was, as far as I remember, it was not even a real theater.
But it was in the house where the firemen had, you know, their house, they used to allow them to make performances.

So I remember that I saw many, many Yiddish shows, like Ansky's And in fact, I was telling my children about the story, because there was a scene that stuck in my mind, in particular, that when one, one who is in that play, calls over the man because-- and says to him, 'Look through the window.
What do you see?' And he said, 'People.' 'Look in the mirror.
What do you see?' He says, 'Myself.' He said, 'It's the same piece of glass.
But as soon as there is a little silver on it, you only see yourself.'

And that was in reference, because the story was about two friends who started in the yeshiva together, and they pledged-- both wives were pregnant-- that if one has a daughter and one a son, that they're going to be, going to marry each other.
In our tradition, we don't, not allowed to make promises like this, because you cannot make a promise on something that is not born.
It's like, you know, like, well, I have a saying. 'The past is history, the present is news, and the future is still unborn.' So you don't give such promises.
And it so happened that one has a son and one a daughter.
However, the one who had the daughter became extremely rich, and he didn't keep his promise.
He completely forgot the promise.
And the whole tragedy comes [INAUDIBLE].
And I'm not going to go into the whole play.
But it was a play by, by Ansky's that I played.
And when I told it to my children, and they went to see it, they said, 'Mom, you were a little girl.
How do you remember?' I said, 'Well, I guess God blessed me with a good memory.' Which is a blessing, and also a curse sometimes, because I remember what I had and what I lost.
So in a way, it's good.
Now, what type of religious background, upbringing did you have?
Well, my, my little brother started to go to Hebrew when he was only three years old, because that was required for boys in-- I started to go, I must have been about six years or so, started to go.
They had [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call it.
Here, it would be called [NON-ENGLISH], you know, girl for school.
And to tell you the truth, I was a very good student in public school.
I liked when they talked about Yiddish history and everything.

But when they spoke in Hebrew, and I was kind always resisted to accept something that I couldn't understand, which I realize now was a mistake, because if I would have learned the words, later if I would get older I would learn the meaning.
But I had from the home, you know, because we observed the, all the Yiddish holidays and had the kashrut and had all the, you know, Jewish history and everything.
And so I've learned to see it.
I spoke fluently Yiddish.
I still do.
At home with my husband, I only speak Yiddish, till this day and with most of my friends.
And Yiddish literature-- in fact, I have a story, which is really amazing.
You know, we didn't have, like we have now, built-in closets, you know, like, sliding doors.
What we had was called a French armoire.
You know, it's like a-- it was a chest, a tall chest, that reached almost from the floor to the ceiling.
And it had two doors.
When you faced it, this, the right-hand side was a little bit narrower, broader than the other one.
One was usually used for the wardrobe.
And the other side, it was linen, nicely-pressed linen, and with ribbons around, and the very highest had shelf in it.
For some reason, when I was a little girl, the door where the wardrobe was supposed to be was always locked.
And I couldn't figure out why.
So I used to play all kinds of games.
You know, I was a very imaginative child.
You know, since earliest childhood, I had a wonderful imagination.
And I was a curious child, and my family always encouraged that curiosity.
And I used to think, guessing games-- maybe a skeleton, maybe a treasure.
I even went as far as to think maybe I was adopted.
I couldn't understand, why is it locked?
And one time, my mother said, 'Itkele, please come.
I want to show you something.' I was trembling with fear and excited with anticipation simultaneously, wondering what is in that closet here and what's so important?

My mother took the key, opened the closet, and there was a whole chest-- it was a closet, was that [INAUDIBLE] of the chest, full with books-- poetry books and, and other books, you know, beautiful bound books.
And my mother pointed to the books, took out the book, and she said to me, 'Itkele, when you're going to learn how to read, you're going to find a treasure here.
You can sit right here in Ciechonw.
You will travel through the world, through the ages, with the greatest mind, everything.' She made it so amazing.
And the reason why she had it locked when we were very little is because I was the oldest of three children, and I was still a little girl.
So she was afraid that the little children might damage the book.
Later on, when we became older, it was open.
And when I learned to-- eagerly to read Yiddish, I was devouring the Yiddish.
And she read me all kinds of stories.
But one story that really became, later, relevant to my life was the story called [NON-ENGLISH].
She read it in Yiddish.
It's called It was written by a Yiddish, Leib Peretz.
And briefly, I'd like to share that story, because it was very relevant later to my life.
I was thinking about it a lot during the concentration camp.
It was about a boy, whose name was Bontsha, but, unfortunately, lost his mother when he was a little child.
His father took another wife when he was 13 years old, which was a very cruel woman and constantly abused Bontsha.
She would begrudge him the food while she ate very well.
She would not give him clothes.
He never had a friend.
He was lonely.
And the coldest winter time, she would chase him out barefoot to chop wood, and all those things.
And she-- and Bontsha was suffering.
But he never complained.
He was always silent.
And that's why the [INAUDIBLE] called him Bontsha the silent.
And he-- not even to his father would he complain.

And one time-- I'm just going to make it brief because it's-- the-- in the coldest winter night, in a drunken stupor, his father threw him out in the cold night, dragged him by the head, and threw him out.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And he was-- and he was-- he got himself up, and he went off wherever his eyes took him.
And he was traveling, and then he came in in a big city.
And when he came in there, he was looking for a job, and he became a porter, and again, was abused constantly.
Again, he was silent.
Then-- I'm going, like, quickly through it.
Then a woman who had a child that was not his child, an infant, that he married.
And he took the infant when she ran off.
She left him alone.
And when the infant became 15 years old, he threw out his-- the man who raised him and abused him again.
And on and on, till finally one day, he was run over by a horse and carriage.
And he, whatever happened, he never complained, never raised his voice-- not to God, not to man.
His suffering was more terrible than Job's, but never complained.
That's-- and then finally, even in the hospital, when he lie in the hospital, he, where everybody is allowed to scream and he wasn't treated well because had not enough money to pay.
And in silence, he lived.
In silence, he died.
And when they buried him, and they put the piece of wood, a woman, the gravedigger's wife, the wind blew away the piece of wood.
And nobody knew where he was, who was laying where.
That this was Bontsha.
And she used it to cook her potatoes.

And in the book, the story said that if he had a name, even if the 100 years, maybe somebody would say that 'Here lays Bontsha.' In this way, he vanished, like he would never live, even like a grain of sand.
And then, when he came to the next world, he was-- everybody knew-- they treated him well.
And they said, 'In the other world where you came from, there is no justice.
But in this world is justice.
And anything you want is yours.'
And he couldn't believe.
Why would somebody be so nice here if nobody was nice there?
And he said, '[NON-ENGLISH]?
Really?

Really?' In Yiddish, [NON-ENGLISH] is 'really.' And he said, 'Yes.' So after giving it a lot of thought, he said, 'Well, in that case, what I would wish, your excellency, is every morning for breakfast a hot roll with butter.'
And when I was in Auschwitz, I was thinking, the biggest millionaire couldn't have a roll, a hot roll with butter.
To have some bread, I understood at that time, the story.
I also understood that six million have no graves and no markers, just like Bontsha [NON-ENGLISH].
And I understood something much deeper than that.
To me, even though my mother didn't say this, because when she-- but I felt-- we would have had a homeland.
We wouldn't be an orphan like Bontsha [NON-ENGLISH].
Nobody would keep us chasing from place to place, and even if we lived for so many years.
My family, on my mother's side, came to Poland in the 1600 century from Germany.
And four generations were still alive, you know, when they came in.
And when we left there-- and I never will remember a remark that somebody made.
A non-Jewish child said to me, 'The houses are yours, but the streets are ours.'
And after living so many years, and not to be a trace left, that is something.
That's why of all the stories, this one had the greatest impact, because I didn't know that one day I will be an orphan and will starved and tortured.
But what I remember, at that time, when I asked my mother, 'How could Bontsha endure so much suffering?'
And my mother, may she rest in peace, answered, in Yiddish, '[YIDDISH]'-- and I'll explain it, what it means in English. '[YIDDISH].
Sometimes a man is stronger than iron, and other times, weaker than a fly.
But,' she said, 'we never know our strength till we haven't been tested.'
